key_quotes: My dad works at Stanford and I live like a block away from campus
it won't be in sunny Palo Alto or experiencing the residential colleges at Yale or Rice
enjoy berkeley while you can! oh and additional bad thing about stanford - we have a quarter system so we don't have dead week
key_points: Almost no student discussion of dorms or residential life exists.
One indirect mention: a student lives a block from campus.
No complaints or praise about Stanford housing appear in data.

key_quotes: Relaxed on the surface but people are working just as hard under the surface, it's called the duck phenomenon (for Stanford)
Can confirm as a high school student nearby, fountain hopping is the best. Especially on XC runs
Social life & having fun are important to me!
key_points: Relaxed vibe is deceptive — students work intensely beneath the surface.
The 'duck phenomenon' is explicitly named by students themselves.
Fountain hopping is a genuine, locally beloved campus activity.

key_quotes: the Stanford dining options are delicious. They've got waffles, soft-serve ice cream, pasta and all kinds of vegan shit that actually taste good
Outside dining is expensive as heck
If you are rich, then there are some nice ass restaurants to go to, there's a fricking Nobu in downtown Palo Alto
key_points: Dining halls praised as delicious and varied by locals.
Options include waffles, soft-serve, pasta, and strong vegan choices.
Off-campus Palo Alto dining is expensive; Nobu is nearby.

key_quotes: Relaxed on the surface but people are working just as hard under the surface, it's called the duck phenomenon (for Stanford)
Palo Alto ----- Silicon Valley big daddy model / Gunn ----- The other Big Daddy model
This admissions cycle was a very difficult, emotionally draining process for me
key_points: Duck syndrome signals intense hidden stress beneath calm exteriors.
Stanford draws from already high-pressure Palo Alto feeder high schools.
No student mental health support resources are mentioned at all.

key_quotes: Harvard University [ full ride ] / Princeton University [ full ride ] / Yale University [ full ride ]
Both schools cost around the same for me, so that is not a factor
I would give my left arm to go to Stanford
key_points: Stanford is never cited as a financial aid leader.
Harvard, Princeton, Yale get praised for full rides — Stanford doesn't.
No specific Stanford aid packages are mentioned by any student.

key_quotes: Stanford is genuinely much stronger than Yale for the 'T' and 'E' parts of STEM
enjoy berkeley while you can! oh and additional bad thing about stanford - we have a quarter system so we don't have dead week
There is a great deal of deeptech coming out of Stanford. The three heavyweights in that arena are Stanford, MIT, and Berkeley
key_points: Stanford is top-tier for CS, engineering, and entrepreneurship.
Seen as genuinely superior to Yale for STEM's 'T' and 'E'.
Quarter system con: no dead week, relentlessly fast pace.

key_quotes: An assistant director of admissions at Stanford University is facing charges of attempted murder and domestic violence in what is believed to be a drug-fueled attack
and that's why elite college admissions sucks... it's so random and based on chance and luck
Your fate is determined by how the AO felt at that particular moment
key_points: An admissions officer was arrested for attempted murder while on LSD.
Students used the incident to joke admissions is pure luck.
No direct commentary on bureaucracy or student services appears.

key_quotes: Campus is really beautiful, lots of trees and grass and Spanish architecture
the weather here from fall-spring is a consistent 65-75 except for the summers where it's like 90-100
Palo Alto is a con... tbh it's really not far from sf or sj
key_points: Campus is consistently called beautiful: Spanish architecture, trees, perfect weather.
Palo Alto is expensive but an hour from San Francisco by Caltrain.
Some students list 'Palo Alto' as a con — feels suburban and isolated.

key_quotes: Stanford, MIT, and Berkeley — the three heavyweights in that arena [deeptech]
next to a (albeit very expensive) downtown area and an hour caltrain ride to San Francisco... perfectly situated for tech internships
UIUC >>> UPenn for a career in silicon valley
key_points: Stanford's tech and startup outcomes are treated as unquestionable.
Cited alongside MIT as the top destination for Silicon Valley careers.
Alumni network and proximity to tech industry are massive structural advantages.

key_quotes: Both schools cost around the same for me, so that is not a factor
Outside dining is expensive as heck
If you are rich, then there are some nice ass restaurants to go to
key_points: Stanford's high cost is assumed but rarely directly discussed.
No financial aid generosity highlighted unlike Harvard, Princeton, Yale.
Consensus: worth it for STEM careers, but financial picture stays murky.

Stanford University = STFU / UC Palo Alto / St. Anford
key_points: Stanford holds near-mythological status as the ultimate tech and STEM school.
Hidden intensity masks a beautiful, chill-seeming campus culture.
Affectionately nicknamed 'St. Anford' and 'UC Palo Alto' by students.

key_quotes: An assistant director of admissions at Stanford University is facing charges of attempted murder and domestic violence in what is believed to be a drug-fueled attack

all kinds of vegan shit that actually taste good

Dining hall quality — especially vegan options — is surprisingly excellent.
Stanford leads in deeptech and hardware startups, not just software.
